title: Spinning fine-weight yarn for lace
subtitle: Producing consistent, high-twist singles suitable for knitting or weaving lace
excerpt: Lace-weight yarn requires a finer draft and more twist per inch than everyday yarn. Small inconsistencies that are invisible in bulky work show clearly at lace weight. This entry covers the wheel settings, fibre choices, and drafting technique needed for consistent fine singles.
sourceNotes: Fine-weight spinning for lace synthesised from general handspinning references. Lace-weight yarn production is treated in detail in Judith MacKenzie McCuin, The Intentional Spinner (2009).

glossaryTerms.definition: A very fine yarn category, typically 600-900 metres per 100 g, used for open-pattern knitting and fine weaving. At lace weight, the holes in the pattern are a structural feature, not a flaw.
The number of complete twists in one inch of yarn, measured by counting untwists in a marked length. Fine yarn requires a higher twist per inch than bulky yarn to hold the fibres together.
A spinning method in which the drafting hand draws back against the incoming twist, keeping the fibre bundle smooth and compact. Worsted draw produces an even, dense singles.
Stretching and pinning a finished lace item while wet to open the pattern and even out the stitches or picks. Blocking transforms uneven handspun lace into a smooth, open fabric.

aliases: spinning thin yarn for lace shawl

content.text: singles demands more consistency than almost any other spinning task. The yarn will eventually be blocked flat, and any thick-thin variation reads as an uneven pattern repeat rather than rustic character. The key adjustments are a higher
, and a fine-staple, well-prepared fibre source. The singles can be knitted or woven as a single thread in many lace patterns; for extra strength they may be plied into a two-ply before

Merino (17-19 micron), Bluefaced Leicester, and fine silk blends are the most reliable choices. The fibre must be a combed top or combed preparation: carded rolags introduce too much air and variation at lace weight. Avoid fleece with any vm (vegetable matter), as fragments are impossible to pick out once the yarn is spun fine.
Step 1: Set the wheel for high twist

Use the smallest drive whorl on the wheel to achieve the highest available twist ratio. Set a light uptake tension: the yarn should be drawn onto the bobbin slowly so twist has time to accumulate in the drafting zone. Spin a short test length and count the twist per inch before committing to a full bobbin. Aim for 20-28 twists per inch for singles that will be plied, or 16-20 twists per inch for singles used as a single-thread lace yarn.

Pull the combed top apart lengthwise into a narrower strip before spinning. For lace weight, the pre-drafted strip should be approximately pencil-diameter. Consistent pre-drafting produces consistent yarn: if the top varies in width before you begin, the singles will vary in diameter. Work through the full top before sitting at the wheel if possible.
Step 3: Spin using a short worsted draw

Hold the pre-drafted top in the back hand and draft from the fibre supply with the front hand. Keep the back hand close to the orifice: no more than 5-6 cm of fibre should be between the hands at any time. Draw back steadily against the incoming twist. Do not let twist run into the unspun fibre zone: this creates a spiral that cannot be corrected. Treadle at a consistent rhythm and draft at a matching pace.

Wind the singles onto the niddy-noddy and tie loosely in four places. Soak in warm water for 10 minutes and hang to dry under light tension. Once dry, measure the twist per inch and weigh a 100 m length to calculate the meterage per 100 g. Lace weight runs 600-900 m per 100 g; if the sample is heavier than this, pre-draft the top more finely on the next spin.

items.problem: The singles break repeatedly during spinning
items.cause: The twist per inch is too low for the diameter being spun
items.fix: Increase the twist ratio by moving to a smaller whorl, or slow the drafting hand to allow more twist to accumulate before drawing back. Always test with a short length before committing a full bobbin.

items.problem: The yarn has thick and thin sections even after careful pre-drafting
items.cause: Fibre supply variation in the combed top, or inconsistent drafting speed
items.fix: Pre-draft the top again to a more even strip. Slow the treadle speed so the drafting motion can be more controlled. Consistent rhythm matters more than fast output.

items.problem: The lace pattern is distorted after knitting and the yarn looks uneven
items.cause: The singles are too low-twist to hold their structure under the tension of knitting needles
items.fix: Ply the singles into a two-ply before knitting: plied lace yarn is more stable under needle tension and blocks more evenly. Alternatively, increase twist per inch and respin.
